description.en: Wire GCP together with events: Pub/Sub topics, subscriptions, filters and dead-letter queues, plus Cloud Run functions and Eventarc as the glue code layer.

story_variants.en:
## From Request-Driven to Event-Driven

Cloud Run answered 'a user is waiting for a response.' This lesson answers the other half of enterprise architecture: 'something happened, and several systems care.' A file lands in a bucket and must be thumbnailed, virus-scanned, and indexed. An order is placed and billing, inventory, and analytics all need to know. Coupling the producer to every consumer creates a distributed monolith; **Pub/Sub** decouples them.

## Pub/Sub: The Nervous System

Pub/Sub is a global, serverless message bus. The vocabulary:

- **Topic** — a named channel producers publish to.
- **Subscription** — a named cursor into a topic. Each subscription receives **every** message published after it exists (fan-out happens per subscription, not per subscriber process).
- **Ack** — a subscriber confirms processing; unacked messages are redelivered after the ack deadline.
- **Push vs pull** — push subscriptions POST messages to an HTTPS endpoint (perfect for Cloud Run); pull subscribers call home for batches (better for high-throughput workers).

Delivery is **at-least-once** by default: duplicates are a fact of life, so handlers must be **idempotent** (dedupe on message ID or a business key). Exactly-once delivery exists for pull subscriptions within a region, and **ordering keys** give per-key in-order delivery — but both trade throughput for guarantees, so reach for them deliberately.

| Feature | What it does | Enterprise use |
|---|---|---|
| Subscription filter | Delivers only messages whose attributes match | One topic, many purpose-scoped consumers |
| Dead-letter topic | Moves messages after N failed deliveries | Quarantine poison messages instead of infinite retry |
| Retry policy | Immediate vs exponential backoff | Backoff protects struggling downstreams |
| Message retention / seek + replay | Keep acked messages, rewind a subscription | Reprocess after a bad deploy |
| BigQuery / GCS subscriptions | Write messages straight to a table or bucket | Zero-code analytics ingestion |

> **Pitfall:** A subscription with no consumer silently accumulates backlog for the retention period. Alert on `oldest_unacked_message_age` — it is the single most predictive Pub/Sub health metric.

## Functions: Glue Code, Managed

Cloud Run functions (the current generation of Cloud Functions, now built on the Cloud Run platform) let you deploy a single function instead of a container. You write `def handle(cloud_event):`, Google builds the image via buildpacks, and the runtime handles scaling exactly like Cloud Run — because it *is* Cloud Run underneath.

Triggers come from **Eventarc**, which normalizes events from 90+ sources into CloudEvents: a GCS object finalized, a Pub/Sub message, an Audit Log entry (e.g. 'someone created a BigQuery dataset'), Firestore document changes. Under the hood most Eventarc triggers are themselves Pub/Sub topics — it's Pub/Sub all the way down.

When functions versus full Cloud Run services?

- Single-purpose event handler under a few hundred lines? **Function.**
- Needs a custom runtime, sidecar-ish processes, or your own Dockerfile? **Cloud Run service.**
- Long batch jobs? **Cloud Run jobs** — functions have request timeouts, not hours.

> **Callout:** The failure mode of event-driven glue is invisible partial failure. Always configure a dead-letter topic on production subscriptions and alert on its depth; an empty DLQ is the cheapest SLO you'll ever run.

## Designing the Fan-Out

The canonical pattern this lesson's challenge simulates: one `orders` topic, three subscriptions —

- `audit-all`: no filter; an append-only compliance archive (often a BigQuery subscription).
- `billing-only`: filter `attributes.dept = "billing"`; drives invoicing.
- `eu-events`: filter `attributes.region = "eu"`; feeds EU-resident processing.

Filters run **inside Pub/Sub**, so the billing consumer never even receives non-billing traffic — cheaper and safer than filtering in code, and unfilterable messages can't crash a consumer that never sees them. Note two real constraints: filters match **attributes** (not the message body), and a subscription's filter cannot be changed after creation — you create a new subscription and migrate.

Schema discipline matters as much as routing. Pub/Sub supports **schemas** (Avro or protobuf) attached to topics so producers can't publish garbage. In practice, teams version events (`orders.v1`) and add fields additively — renaming a field in a live event stream is a multi-team incident wearing a trench coat.

## Where This Meets ML

Event-driven patterns recur throughout the Vertex AI half of this track: a GCS trigger kicks off a pipeline run when new training data lands; model-monitoring alerts publish to a topic that pages the on-call and opens a ticket; batch prediction completions fan out to downstream refreshes. The `orders` topic you simulate today is structurally identical to the `ml-events` topic you'll wire next month.

## What You'll Build

A deterministic fan-out simulator: three subscriptions (one unfiltered, two attribute-filtered), three published messages, and a delivery count per subscription plus a total. It forces the two insights people usually learn in production: fan-out multiplies delivery volume (and cost), and filters are evaluated per subscription against attributes only.

description.en: Every subscription gets its own copy of the stream; filters trim each copy independently.

left.label: Deliver everything, filter inside consumer code

right.label: Subscription filters on attributes — unwanted messages never delivered

description.en: Server-side filtering cuts delivery cost and removes a whole class of consumer crashes.

animation_sequence.code:
from google.cloud import pubsub_v1
pub = pubsub_v1.PublisherClient()
topic = pub.topic_path("proj", "orders")
future = pub.publish(topic, b'{"id": 42}',
                     dept="billing", region="eu")
print(future.result())

explanation.en: The payload is bytes; Pub/Sub does not care what's inside.
Keyword args become attributes — the only thing subscription filters can match.
publish() is async; result() blocks until the server assigns a message ID.

lines: $ gcloud monitoring metrics list | grep oldest_unacked

description.en: The two production guardrails: a dead-letter topic and an alert on backlog age.
The at-least-once lifecycle; the DLQ branch is what saves your 3 a.m.

instruction.en: Simulate Pub/Sub fan-out with subscription filters. SUBS maps subscription name to a filter: None (deliver all) or an (attribute_key, required_value) tuple. Implement matches(filter_spec, attrs) returning True when the filter is None or attrs[key] == value, and deliver(subs, messages) returning a dict of subscription -> delivered count. Then print one line per subscription in sorted name order: '<name>: <count> messages', followed by a final line 'total deliveries: <sum>'. Print nothing else.

practice_challenges.expected_output:
audit-all: 3 messages
billing-only: 2 messages
eu-events: 2 messages
total deliveries: 7

en: matches: return filter_spec is None or attrs.get(filter_spec[0]) == filter_spec[1].
deliver: nested loop — for each sub, count messages where matches(...) is True.
Iterate sorted(counts) for stable output order, then sum(counts.values()) for the total.

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert matches(None, {"dept": "x"}) is True
assert matches(("dept", "billing"), {"dept": "billing", "region": "us"}) is True
assert matches(("region", "eu"), {"dept": "billing", "region": "us"}) is False
_c = deliver(SUBS, MESSAGES)
assert _c == {"audit-all": 3, "billing-only": 2, "eu-events": 2}
assert sum(_c.values()) == 7

q.en: Two subscriptions exist on one topic. A message is published. Who receives it?

en: Each subscription gets its own copy (subject to its filter)
It is load-balanced between the subscriptions

q.en: Pub/Sub's default delivery guarantee means your handler must be:

en: Idempotent — duplicates can and will arrive

q.en: What do subscription filters match against?

en: The JSON body of the message

q.en: A consumer keeps crashing on one malformed message, blocking progress. The right guardrail is:

en: Increase the ack deadline to a week
Configure a dead-letter topic with max delivery attempts
